notes.note: Sextilis was the sixth month of the old Roman year (later renamed August); its Ides fell on the 13th. The Roman calendar counted days backward from the Kalends, Nones, and Ides, so "the day before the Ides" is the 12th.

Pollex, a slave of Cicero's used as agent and courier; Cicero puns on his name, which means "thumb," against index, the "pointing" forefinger --- the man turned up but pointed nothing out. He is the agent who must enter on the Cluvius inheritance.

notes.anchor: the one he handed over to Lepidus
notes.note: M. Aemilius Lepidus, Caesar's master of the horse and the future triumvir, who had received from Balbus a property near Lanuvium. Balbus's largesse to such men is the backdrop to Cicero's own dealings with him.

Cicero's Cato, a laudatory pamphlet (the Laus Catonis) written in 45 BC praising Cato Uticensis. Caesar read it repeatedly; Caesar himself answered it with an Anticato. No separate text entity is registered.
Brutus likewise composed his own Cato in praise of his uncle; Caesar reports that reading it made him feel eloquent by contrast. No separate text entity is registered.

M. Cluvius, a wealthy banker of Puteoli who made Cicero an heir; Cicero is arranging the formal acceptance of the bequest. The associated "Cluvian gardens" were among the properties Cicero hoped to gain.

notes.anchor: formal acceptance, with witnesses present, within sixty days
notes.note: The cretio: a Roman heir had to declare acceptance of an inheritance by a set formula before witnesses, within a fixed period (here sixty days), or forfeit the bequest. Cicero must send an agent to perform it on his behalf.

notes.anchor: a legacy from T. Hordeonius and to Terentia 50,000 sesterces
notes.note: Cluvius's will charged his estate with legacies --- one connected with Titus Hordeonius and 50,000 sesterces to Terentia, Cicero's former wife --- but, to Cicero's relief, nothing burdening his own share. The sestertius was the standard Roman unit of account.

An unguentarius (dealer in perfumed oils) named Plotius, whose slaves had carried news to Balbus faster than Cicero's own people brought it to him; the point is the speed of his private courier network, not the man himself. No entity is bound.
Cicero mourns the recent death of Cossinius, a friend (most likely the Roman eques L. Cossinius linked to Atticus's circle). The binding is by best registry fit and should be confirmed.
